title: Euler path Hierholzer
description: Find Euler path using Hierholzer algorithm

examples.explanation: Step 1: Check degrees - all vertices have degree 2 (even), Euler circuit exists. Step 2: Start at vertex 0. Step 3: Initialize used_edges={}, path=[0]. Step 4: From 0, available edges: (0,1) and (0,2). Try (0,1): mark used, path=[0,1], current=1. From 1, available: (1,2). Try (1,2): mark used, path=[0,1,2], current=2. From 2, available: (2,0). Try (2,0): mark used, path=[0,1,2,0], current=0. All edges used, return path.

Step 1: Check degrees: 0:3, 1:2, 2:3, 3:2. Two vertices (0,2) have odd degree, Euler path exists. Step 2: Start at vertex 0 (odd degree). Step 3: used_edges={}, path=[0]. Step 4: From 0, edges: (0,1),(0,2),(0,3). Try (0,1): used, path=[0,1], current=1. From 1, edge (1,2): used, path=[0,1,2], current=2. From 2, edges: (2,0),(2,3). Try (2,3): used, path=[0,1,2,3], current=3. From 3, edge (3,0): used, path=[0,1,2,3,0], current=0. From 0, remaining edge (0,2): used, path=[0,1,2,3,0,2], current=2. All edges used, return path.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS Backtracking
approaches.summary: Explore all possible permutations of edges via depth-first search with backtracking to find a valid Euler path, checking Eulerian conditions (degree parity) upfront. Use this only for small graphs due to factorial time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation exploring all edge permutations via DFS backtracking to find Euler path after verifying Eulerian conditions.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Euler path problem by first checking necessary Eulerian conditions (degree parity), then performing exhaustive DFS backtracking through all possible edge permutations. The intuition is that for small graphs, we can brute-force search all possible paths by trying edges in all orders, backtracking when we hit dead ends. This works because an Euler path must use all edges exactly once, so we're essentially searching all permutations of edges. We start from a valid starting vertex (odd degree vertex if exists, otherwise any vertex), then recursively explore edges. At each step, we mark an edge as used, add it to the path, and continue. If we reach a state where no unused edges remain from current vertex but some edges remain unused elsewhere, we backtrack. The algorithm terminates when we find a path using all edges or exhaust all possibilities. Time complexity is O(n!) where n is number of edges, as we explore all permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and tracking used edges. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS backtracking subroutine as its core search mechanism. Common mistakes include not checking Eulerian conditions first (leading to wasted computation), improper backtracking (forgetting to unmark edges), and not handling disconnected graphs correctly. Real code pattern: def find_euler_path_bruteforce(graph): 1. Check degree parity conditions 2. Find start vertex 3. Initialize used_edges set and path list 4. Define dfs_backtrack(current_vertex) function 5. Call dfs_backtrack(start_vertex) 6. Return path if found, else None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check degrees - all vertices have degree 2 (even), Euler circuit exists. Step 2: Start at vertex 0. Step 3: Initialize used_edges={}, path=[0]. Step 4: From 0, available edges: (0,1) and (0,2). Try (0,1): mark used, path=[0,1], current=1. From 1, available: (1,2). Try (1,2): mark used, path=[0,1,2], current=2. From 2, available: (2,0). Try (2,0): mark used, path=[0,1,2,0], current=0. All edges used, return path.

Step 1: Check degrees: 0:3, 1:2, 2:3, 3:2. Two vertices (0,2) have odd degree, Euler path exists. Step 2: Start at vertex 0 (odd degree). Step 3: used_edges={}, path=[0]. Step 4: From 0, edges: (0,1),(0,2),(0,3). Try (0,1): used, path=[0,1], current=1. From 1, edge (1,2): used, path=[0,1,2], current=2. From 2, edges: (2,0),(2,3). Try (2,3): used, path=[0,1,2,3], current=3. From 3, edge (3,0): used, path=[0,1,2,3,0], current=0. From 0, remaining edge (0,2): used, path=[0,1,2,3,0,2], current=2. All edges used, return path.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal / Bridge Detection
ladders.desc: Depth-first search with backtracking that explores all possible orders of using edges from a starting vertex.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs the core search of the brute force approach. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible paths by recursively trying each available edge, backtracking when we reach a dead end. At each vertex, we consider all unused edges incident to it. For each such edge, we mark it as used, move to the adjacent vertex, and recurse. If recursion returns a complete path using all edges, we return it. Otherwise, we unmark the edge (backtrack) and try the next edge. The algorithm ensures we explore all permutations of edge usage. Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(n!) in worst case as it explores all edge permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and tracking used edges. Connection to L0: This is the main search engine called after verifying Eulerian conditions. Connection to L2: This implements the permutation exploration technique. Common mistakes include not properly backtracking (forgetting to unmark edges), not checking if all edges are used before returning, and inefficient edge lookup. Real code pattern: def dfs_backtrack(current, used_edges, path, total_edges): if len(used_edges) == total_edges: return path for edge in get_incident_edges(current): if edge not in used_edges: used_edges.add(edge); path.append(edge[1]); result = dfs_backtrack(edge[1], used_edges, path, total_edges); if result: return result; path.pop(); used_edges.remove(edge) return None

examples.input: Start vertex: 0, edges from 0: (0,1),(0,2), total edges: 3, used: {}
examples.output: Path using all edges or None
examples.trace: Step 1: current=0, used={}, path=[0]. Step 2: Try edge (0,1): used={(0,1)}, path=[0,1], recurse to vertex 1. Step 3: At vertex 1, available edges: (1,0),(1,2). Try (1,0): used={(0,1),(1,0)}, path=[0,1,0], recurse to 0. Step 4: At vertex 0, available: (0,2). Try (0,2): used={(0,1),(1,0),(0,2)}, path=[0,1,0,2], recurse to 2. Step 5: At vertex 2, no edges available but used has 3 edges = total, return path.

Step 1: current=0, used={}, path=[0]. Step 2: Try (0,1): used={(0,1)}, path=[0,1], recurse to 1. Step 3: At 1, try (1,2): used={(0,1),(1,2)}, path=[0,1,2], recurse to 2. Step 4: At 2, try (2,0): used={(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)}, path=[0,1,2,0], all edges used, return path.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Circuit Merging / Edge Selection
ladders.desc: The technique of systematically generating and testing all possible orders in which edges can be traversed.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the idea of treating edge traversal as a permutation problem. The intuition is that an Euler path must use each edge exactly once, so the order of edge usage forms a permutation of the edge set. We need to find a permutation where consecutive edges share vertices appropriately. The technique works by: 1) Starting from a valid vertex, 2) At each step, choosing an unused incident edge, 3) Recursing to the next vertex, 4) Backtracking if we reach a vertex with no unused incident edges but other edges remain unused. This explores all n! possible permutations in worst case. Time complexity is O(n!) due to permutation generation. Space complexity is O(n) for tracking the current permutation and used edges. Connection to L1: This is the conceptual foundation that the DFS backtracking implements. Connection to L3: This relies on graph traversal to explore edges. Common mistakes include not considering all permutations (e.g., due to pruning too early), confusing edge permutations with vertex permutations, and not handling parallel edges correctly. Real code pattern: The permutation exploration is embodied in the recursive choice: for each unused edge e incident to current vertex v: choose e, mark used, recurse from other endpoint of e, if fails: unchoose e. This generates all sequences where edges are used exactly once.

examples.trace: Permutation 1: Use (0,1) first, then (1,2) -> path [0,1,2]. Permutation 2: Cannot use (1,2) first from 0, so only one valid permutation. The algorithm tries: Step 1: At 0, only edge (0,1). Use it, go to 1. Step 2: At 1, edge (1,2). Use it, go to 2. Step 3: No edges left, success.

Valid permutations include: 1) (0,1),(1,2),(2,0) -> [0,1,2,0]. 2) (0,2),(2,1),(1,0) -> [0,2,1,0]. The algorithm explores: From 0, try (0,1): leads to permutation 1. Backtrack, try (0,2): leads to permutation 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Construction & Degrees
ladders.desc: Traversing a graph while keeping track of which edges have been used.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves moving through a graph from vertex to vertex via edges, while maintaining a set of used edges to ensure we don't reuse them. The intuition is that to find an Euler path, we need to walk through the graph using each edge exactly once, which requires both movement between vertices and memory of which edges we've already taken. The algorithm: 1) Start at a vertex, 2) Look at all edges incident to the current vertex, 3) Filter to only unused edges, 4) Choose one, mark it as used, move to its other endpoint, 5) Repeat. This is similar to DFS but with edge-based rather than vertex-based visitation. Time complexity for traversal depends on graph representation; with adjacency list it's O(V+E) for a complete traversal if we don't backtrack. Space complexity is O(E) for tracking used edges. Connection to L2: This provides the mechanism to explore edge permutations by enabling movement through the graph. Connection to L4: This uses basic operations of edge lookup and marking. Common mistakes include using vertex visitation instead of edge visitation (allowing edge reuse), inefficient edge lookup (scanning all edges each time), and not handling multigraphs correctly. Real code pattern: def traverse_with_edge_tracking(start, graph): used = set(); current = start; while True: available = [e for e in graph[current] if e not in used]; if not available: break; edge = available[0]; used.add(edge); current = edge[1] (other endpoint). This simple version doesn't backtrack but shows the core idea.

examples.trace: Step 1: current=0, used={}. Available edges from 0: (0,1),(0,2). Choose (0,1), mark used={(0,1)}, current=1. Step 2: current=1, available: (1,0) not in used. Choose (1,0), used={(0,1),(1,0)}, current=0. Step 3: current=0, available: (0,2) not in used. Choose (0,2), used={(0,1),(1,0),(0,2)}, current=2. Step 4: current=2, available: (2,0) not in used. Choose (2,0), used={(0,1),(1,0),(0,2),(2,0)}, current=0. Step 5: current=0, no available edges, stop.
Step 1: current=0, used={}. Available: (0,1). Choose it, used={(0,1)}, current=1. Step 2: current=1, available: (1,2). Choose it, used={(0,1),(1,2)}, current=2. Step 3: current=2, no edges, stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Edge Removal / Traversal
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of testing if an edge has been used and marking it as used.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two key actions: 1) Checking whether a specific edge has already been traversed in the current path, and 2) Marking an edge as used once we traverse it. The intuition is that to ensure each edge is used exactly once (Euler path condition), we need a way to remember which edges we've already taken and avoid reusing them. In implementation, this is typically done with a set data structure where edges are stored as tuples (u,v). Checking is O(1) average case via set lookup, marking is O(1) via set addition. For undirected graphs, we need to store edges in a canonical form (e.g., (min(u,v), max(u,v))) to ensure (u,v) and (v,u) are treated as the same edge. Time complexity for each check/mark is O(1). Space complexity is O(E) for storing all used edges. Connection to L3: This operation enables the edge tracking required for graph traversal. Connection to L5: This builds on the concept of sets for membership testing. Common mistakes include not handling undirected edges correctly (treating (u,v) and (v,u) as different), using a list instead of set (making checks O(n)), and not clearing marks during backtracking. Real code pattern: used_edges = set(); def is_used(edge): return edge in used_edges; def mark_used(edge): used_edges.add(edge); def unmark_used(edge): used_edges.remove(edge). For undirected: canonical_edge = (min(u,v), max(u,v)).

examples.input: Start with empty used set, check edge (0,1), then mark it, then check again
examples.output: First check: False, after marking: True
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize used_edges = set(). Step 2: Check if (0,1) in used_edges: returns False. Step 3: Mark used: used_edges.add((0,1)) -> set contains (0,1). Step 4: Check if (0,1) in used_edges: returns True.

examples.input: Undirected edge between 0 and 1, check both orientations
examples.output: Both (0,1) and (1,0) should be considered used after marking one
examples.trace: Step 1: used_edges = set(). Step 2: Mark canonical edge: used_edges.add((0,1)) since min(0,1)=0, max=1. Step 3: Check (0,1): create canonical (0,1), check in set -> True. Step 4: Check (1,0): create canonical (0,1), check in set -> True.

testCases.expected: Set only has one, but multigraph needs count

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Euler Path & Graph Basics
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, edges, degrees, and Euler paths.
ladders.explanation: An Euler path visits every edge exactly once. It exists if exactly 0 or 2 vertices have odd degree (undirected) or specific indegree/outdegree conditions (directed). Hierholzer finds it by merging circuits efficiently.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize: S = set(). Step 2: Add 1: S = {1}. Step 3: Add 3: S = {1,3}. Step 4: Check if 2 in S: False. Step 5: Add 5: S = {1,3,5}. Step 6: Check if 3 in S: True. Step 7: Remove 1: S = {3,5}. Step 8: Check if 1 in S: False.

Step 1: S = set(). Step 2: Add (0,1): S = {(0,1)}. Step 3: Add (1,2): S = {(0,1),(1,2)}. Step 4: Check if (0,1) in S: True. Step 5: Check if (2,1) in S: False (unless canonical). Step 6: Remove (0,1): S = {(1,2)}.

approaches.name: Hierholzer's Algorithm
approaches.summary: Find Euler path by iteratively constructing circuits using DFS on unused edges and merging them into a single path, starting from a vertex with odd degree if an Euler path exists. This is the standard efficient method for directed or undirected graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Hierholzer's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find an Euler path in a graph using Hierholzer's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Hierholzer algorithm efficiently finds an Euler path by constructing circuits through DFS on unused edges and merging them. An Euler path exists if the graph has exactly 0 or 2 vertices with odd degree (for undirected) or satisfies Euler path conditions for directed graphs. The algorithm starts from a vertex with odd degree (if any) or any vertex if all degrees are even. It uses DFS to find a circuit, backtracking when no unused edges remain, and builds the path in reverse order. The intuition is that any Eulerian graph can be decomposed into edge-disjoint cycles, and the algorithm recursively finds and merges these cycles. Time complexity is O(E) as each edge is processed once, and space is O(E + V) for adjacency lists and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This integrates the DFS subroutine to traverse edges. Common mistakes include not handling multiple edges correctly, forgetting to mark edges as used in undirected graphs (both directions), and incorrect start vertex selection. Real code pattern: Use adjacency lists with edge removal or visited flags, recursion or stack for DFS, and build result in reverse order.

examples.trace: Graph: vertices 0,1,2,3. Degrees: 0:2, 1:4, 2:2, 3:2 (all even, Euler circuit exists). Start at vertex 0. DFS from 0: edge 0-1 unused, go to 1. From 1: edge 1-3 unused, go to 3. From 3: only edge 3-1 unused, go to 1. From 1: edge 1-2 unused, go to 2. From 2: edge 2-0 unused, go to 0. From 0: no unused edges, backtrack. Build path in reverse: at 0, add 0. Back to 2, add 2. Back to 1, add 1. Back to 3, add 3. Back to 1, add 1. Back to 0, add 0. Reverse full: [0,1,3,1,2,0].

Graph: vertices 0,1,2,3. Indegrees: 0:1, 1:2, 2:1, 3:1. Outdegrees: 0:1, 1:2, 2:1, 3:1. All vertices have indegree=outdegree, Euler circuit exists. Start at vertex 0. DFS from 0: edge 0->1 unused, go to 1. From 1: edge 1->3 unused, go to 3. From 3: edge 3->1 unused, go to 1. From 1: edge 1->2 unused, go to 2. From 2: edge 2->0 unused, go to 0. From 0: no unused edges, backtrack. Build reverse: at 0, add 0. Back to 2, add 2. Back to 1, add 1. Back to 3, add 3. Back to 1, add 1. Back to 0, add 0. Reverse full: [0,1,3,1,2,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal / Bridge Detection
ladders.desc: Depth-first search function that traverses unused edges to build a circuit.
ladders.explanation: This DFS subroutine is the core of Hierholzer's algorithm, responsible for exploring edges from a vertex until no unused edges remain, effectively finding a circuit. The intuition is that in an Eulerian graph, from any vertex, you can follow unused edges in a depth-first manner to form a closed loop. The algorithm uses recursion or a stack: at each vertex, while there are unused edges, take one, mark it as used, and recursively call DFS on the adjacent vertex. When no edges remain, the current vertex is added to a path list (in reverse order). This ensures that the circuit is constructed backtracking. Time complexity is O(E) for the entire graph as each edge is visited once, and space is O(V) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main algorithm to build the full Euler path. Common mistakes include not removing edges from adjacency lists efficiently (leading to O(E^2) time), infinite recursion if edges are not marked used, and incorrect order of adding vertices. Real code pattern: Use a while loop over adjacency list, pop edges, and recursive calls.

examples.trace: DFS(0): adjacency[0]=[1], take edge 0->1, mark used, call DFS(1). DFS(1): adjacency[1]=[2], take edge 1->2, mark used, call DFS(2). DFS(2): adjacency[2]=[0], take edge 2->0, mark used, call DFS(0). DFS(0): adjacency[0] is empty, add 0 to path. Backtrack to DFS(2): add 2 to path. Backtrack to DFS(1): add 1 to path. Backtrack to DFS(0): add 0 to path. Path built in reverse: [0,2,1,0].

DFS(1): adjacency[1]=[0,2], take edge 1->0, mark used, call DFS(0). DFS(0): adjacency[0]=[1], take edge 0->1, mark used, call DFS(1). DFS(1): adjacency[1]=[2] (0 removed), take edge 1->2, mark used, call DFS(2). DFS(2): adjacency[2]=[1], take edge 2->1, mark used, call DFS(1). DFS(1): adjacency[1] is empty, add 1 to path. Backtrack to DFS(2): add 2 to path. Backtrack to DFS(1): add 1 to path. Backtrack to DFS(0): add 0 to path. Backtrack to DFS(1): add 1 to path. Path: [1,2,1,0,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Circuit Merging / Edge Selection
ladders.desc: The technique of recursively finding and combining circuits to form the full Euler path.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of Hierholzer's algorithm is the iterative merging of circuits. The intuition is that an Eulerian graph can be decomposed into edge-disjoint cycles, and the Euler path is formed by stitching these cycles together. The algorithm starts DFS from a vertex, and when it backtracks to a vertex that still has unused edges, it initiates a new DFS from there to find another circuit, which is then inserted into the current path. This is managed by the recursive nature: during DFS, if a vertex has more unused edges after returning from a recursive call, the algorithm continues exploring, effectively merging circuits. The key insight is that the path is built in reverse order during backtracking, ensuring that circuits are combined correctly. Time and space are as in L1. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented within the DFS subroutine by handling multiple edges per vertex. Common mistakes include not handling the insertion of new circuits properly, leading to incorrect path order, and not ensuring all edges are used. Real code pattern: In DFS, use a while loop to process all edges of a vertex, recursively calling DFS for each.

examples.trace: DFS(0): adjacency[0]=[1,2], take edge 0->1, call DFS(1). DFS(1): adjacency[1]=[0], take edge 1->0, call DFS(0). DFS(0): adjacency[0]=[2] (1 removed), take edge 0->2, call DFS(2). DFS(2): adjacency[2]=[0], take edge 2->0, call DFS(0). DFS(0): adjacency[0] is empty, add 0. Backtrack: at DFS(2), add 2. At DFS(0), add 0. At DFS(1), add 1. At DFS(0), add 0. Path: [0,2,0,1,0] (circuits merged: 0-1-0 and 0-2-0).

DFS(1): adjacency[1]=[0,2,3], take edge 1->0, call DFS(0). DFS(0): adjacency[0]=[1], take edge 0->1, call DFS(1). DFS(1): adjacency[1]=[2,3] (0 removed), take edge 1->2, call DFS(2). DFS(2): adjacency[2]=[1], take edge 2->1, call DFS(1). DFS(1): adjacency[1]=[3] (2 removed), take edge 1->3, call DFS(3). DFS(3): adjacency[3]=[1], take edge 3->1, call DFS(1). DFS(1): adjacency[1] is empty, add 1. Backtrack: add 3, add 1, add 2, add 1, add 0, add 1. Path: [1,3,1,2,1,0,1] (multiple circuits merged).

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Construction & Degrees
ladders.desc: Construct adjacency list and compute vertex degrees to check Euler path conditions.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing the graph and calculating degrees to determine if an Euler path exists. The intuition is that Euler path existence depends on vertex degrees: for undirected graphs, there must be exactly 0 or 2 vertices with odd degree; for directed graphs, at most one vertex has indegree - outdegree = 1, at most one has outdegree - indegree = 1, and all others have equal indegree and outdegree. The algorithm builds an adjacency list from edge lists, which allows O(1) edge removal during DFS. Degree calculation is done by iterating through edges and incrementing counts. Time complexity is O(E + V) for construction and degree calculation, space is O(E + V) for adjacency list and degree arrays. Connection to L2: This provides the graph structure and start vertex for the DFS logic. Common mistakes include incorrect degree updates for undirected graphs (each edge contributes to both vertices), not handling multiple edges, and misidentifying start vertex. Real code pattern: Use dictionaries or lists for adjacency, and arrays for degrees.

examples.trace: Initialize adjacency dict and degree dict. For edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0], add 0 to adj[1]; increment degree[0] and degree[1] by 1. For [1,2]: add 2 to adj[1], add 1 to adj[2]; increment degree[1] and degree[2] by 1. For [2,0]: add 0 to adj[2], add 2 to adj[0]; increment degree[2] and degree[0] by 1. Final degrees: all 2 (even), so Euler circuit exists, start at vertex 0.

Initialize adjacency dict, indegree and outdegree dicts. For edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0]; increment outdegree[0] by 1, indegree[1] by 1. For [1,2]: add 2 to adj[1]; increment outdegree[1], indegree[2]. For [2,0]: add 0 to adj[2]; increment outdegree[2], indegree[0]. For [1,3]: add 3 to adj[1]; increment outdegree[1], indegree[3]. For [3,1]: add 1 to adj[3]; increment outdegree[3], indegree[1]. Check degrees: all vertices have indegree=outdegree, so Euler circuit exists, start at vertex 0.

testCases.expected: Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler path, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler path, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0
Adjacency and degrees indicate Euler circuit, start:0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Edge Removal / Traversal
ladders.desc: Efficiently remove an edge from an adjacency list during DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves deleting an edge from the adjacency list to mark it as used, preventing re-traversal. The intuition is that to ensure each edge is visited exactly once in O(E) time, we need constant-time removal. For adjacency lists implemented as lists or deques, we can pop from the end (or use pointers) to avoid shifting elements. In undirected graphs, both directions must be removed, which can be managed by using a multiset or by searching in the adjacent list. Time complexity is O(1) per edge removal if implemented with pop, space is O(1) additional. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the graph representation during DFS. Common mistakes include using remove() which is O(n) leading to O(E^2) time, not handling multiple edges correctly, and in undirected graphs, not removing the reverse edge. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list as list of lists, and pop() from the end while iterating.

examples.trace: Start with adjacency dict. To remove edge 0-1: in adj[0], find index of 1 (index 0), pop it (using pop(0) or better, use pop() if order doesn't matter—assume pop last for efficiency: if 1 is last, pop; here 1 is at index 0, so we might swap or use deque). For simplicity, if we pop last, and list is [1,2], pop gives 2, but we want to remove 1—so we need to find and remove. Efficient way: use while loop and pop from end until we find 1, but for this example, remove 1 from adj[0] making it [2]. Also remove reverse edge: in adj[1], remove 0 making it [].
Remove edge 1-2: in adj[1], which is [0,2], remove 2 (if using pop from end and 2 is last, pop it, making adj[1]=[0]). Remove reverse edge: in adj[2], which is [1], remove 1 making it [].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Euler Path & Graph Basics
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, edges, degrees, and Euler paths.
ladders.explanation: An Euler path visits every edge exactly once. It exists if exactly 0 or 2 vertices have odd degree (undirected) or specific indegree/outdegree conditions (directed). Hierholzer finds it by merging circuits efficiently.

examples.output: Euler circuit exists because all vertices have degree 2 (even).
examples.trace: Vertices: 0,1,2. Edges: 0 connected to 1 and 2, so degree 0=2. Vertex 1 connected to 0 and 2, degree 1=2. Vertex 2 connected to 0 and 1, degree 2=2. All degrees are even, so Euler circuit exists (e.g., path 0-1-2-0).

examples.output: Euler path exists because vertices 0 and 2 have odd degree (1 and 1), others have even.
examples.trace: Vertices: 0 connected to 1, degree 0=1 (odd). Vertex 1 connected to 0,2,3, degree 1=3 (odd). Vertex 2 connected to 1,3, degree 2=2 (even). Vertex 3 connected to 1,2, degree 3=2 (even). Two vertices (0 and 1) have odd degree, so Euler path exists (e.g., 0-1-3-2-1).

testCases.expected: Euler path exists (0 and 1 have degree 1, odd)
Euler path exists (0 and 2 have degree 1, odd)
Euler path exists (0 has degree 3, odd; 1 has 2, even; 2 has 3, odd)

approaches.name: Fleury's Algorithm
approaches.summary: Find Euler path by traversing edges one by one, avoiding bridges unless necessary, and removing visited edges. It is slower due to repeated bridge checks but guarantees a valid path if Eulerian conditions hold.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Fleury's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Fleury's algorithm to find an Euler path in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: Fleury's algorithm finds an Euler path by traversing edges one by one while avoiding bridges unless necessary. The intuition is that by always choosing non-bridge edges when possible, we ensure we don't get stuck in disconnected components. The algorithm works because in an Eulerian graph, removing a non-bridge edge maintains connectivity, allowing us to return later to complete the path. Step-by-step: 1) Check Eulerian conditions (0 or 2 vertices with odd degree). 2) Start from a vertex with odd degree (if exists) or any vertex. 3) While unvisited edges remain: a) For current vertex, examine incident edges. b) If there's a non-bridge edge, take it. c) If all incident edges are bridges, take any bridge. d) Mark edge as visited, move to next vertex, add edge to path. 4) Return path. Time: O(E²) due to repeated bridge checks using DFS for each edge selection. Space: O(E+V) for adjacency list and visited tracking. Connection to L1: This integrates the bridge-checking subroutine with the main traversal logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle graphs with no Euler path, incorrect bridge detection, or not updating degrees properly after edge removal. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list with edge tracking, DFS for bridge checking, and stack for path construction.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check degrees: deg(0)=2, deg(1)=3, deg(2)=3, deg(3)=3, deg(4)=1 → 2 odd vertices (1,2) → Euler path exists. Start at vertex 1 (odd degree). Step 2: At vertex 1, edges: (1,0) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0]. Step 3: At vertex 0, edges: (0,2) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0,2]. Step 4: At vertex 2, edges: (2,1) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0,2,1]. Step 5: At vertex 1, edges: (1,3) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0,2,1,3]. Step 6: At vertex 3, edges: (3,2) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0,2,1,3,2]. Step 7: At vertex 2, edges: (2,3) non
... (simplified)

Step 1: Check degrees: deg(0)=2, deg(1)=3, deg(2)=3, deg(3)=2 → 2 odd vertices (1,2) → Euler path exists. Start at vertex 1. Step 2: At vertex 1, edges: (1,0) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0]. Step 3: At vertex 0, edges: (0,2) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0,2]. Step 4: At vertex 2, edges: (2,1) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0,2,1]. Step 5: At vertex 1, edges: (1,3) non-bridge, take it → path: [1,0,2,1,3]. Step 6: At vertex 3, edges: (3,2) only edge, take it → path: [1,0,2,1,3,2]. Step 7: At vertex 2, edges: (2,0) only edge, take it → path:

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal / Bridge Detection
ladders.desc: Determine if an edge is a bridge (cut-edge) in the current graph state.
ladders.explanation: A bridge is an edge whose removal increases the number of connected components. In Fleury's algorithm, we avoid bridges unless necessary to prevent disconnecting the graph. The intuition: removing a bridge splits the graph, potentially leaving some edges unreachable. We detect bridges using DFS: an edge (u,v) is a bridge if there's no back-edge from v's subtree to u or its ancestors. Step-by-step: 1) Perform DFS from current vertex, tracking discovery times and low values. 2) Low[v] = min(discovery time of v, low values of adjacent vertices, discovery times of back-edge ancestors). 3) For edge (u,v), if low[v] > disc[u], then it's a bridge. Time: O(V+E) per check. Space: O(V) for arrays. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly during edge selection. Common mistakes: Not considering graph changes after edge removal, incorrect low value updates, or missing back-edges in undirected graphs. Real code pattern: Use recursion with disc[], low[], parent parameters, and check condition after DFS returns.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0-3, edges: (0,1), (1,2), (2,3), (3,0), current edge (1,2) to check
examples.trace: Step 1: Run DFS from vertex 0. disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. Step 3: From 1, visit 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. Step 4: From 2, visit 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. Step 5: From 3, back-edge to 0: low[3]=min(3,0)=0. Step 6: Backtrack to 2: low[2]=min(2,low[3]=0)=0. Step 7: Backtrack to 1: low[1]=min(1,low[2]=0)=0. Step 8: Check edge (1,2): low[2]=0, disc[1]=1 → low[2] <= disc[1] → not a bridge.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0-3, edges: (0,1), (1,2), (2,3), remove edge (1,2), check if (0,1) is bridge
examples.trace: Step 1: After removing (1,2), graph has edges: (0,1), (2,3). Step 2: Run DFS from vertex 0. disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Step 3: Visit neighbor 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. Step 4: Vertex 1 has no other neighbors (edge to 2 removed). Step 5: Backtrack: low[1]=1. Step 6: Check edge (0,1): low[1]=1, disc[0]=0 → low[1] > disc[0] → it is a bridge.

testCases.input: Graph: 4 vertices, edges: (0,1), (1,2), (2,3), check edge (1,2) after removing (0,1)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Circuit Merging / Edge Selection
ladders.desc: Choose next edge to traverse, preferring non-bridge edges.
ladders.explanation: The core of Fleury's algorithm is selecting which edge to take from the current vertex. The intuition: by avoiding bridges when possible, we ensure the graph remains connected for future traversal. This guarantees we can always return to visit remaining edges. Step-by-step: 1) From current vertex u, list all incident unvisited edges. 2) For each edge (u,v), check if it's a bridge using bridge detection. 3) If any non-bridge edge exists, select one arbitrarily. 4) If all edges are bridges, select any bridge (this happens only when it's the only option). 5) Mark edge as visited and move to v. Time: O(E) per selection in worst case. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L1: Uses bridge detection as a black box. Common mistakes: Selecting bridges unnecessarily when non-bridges exist, not updating visited status correctly, or mishandling multi-edges. Real code pattern: Iterate through adjacency list, call is_bridge() for each edge, and pick first non-bridge or fallback to bridge.

examples.input: Current vertex: 1, unvisited edges: (1,0), (1,2), (1,3). Graph is connected cycle.
examples.trace: Step 1: List edges from vertex 1: (1,0), (1,2), (1,3). Step 2: Check (1,0): not a bridge (part of cycle). Step 3: Since non-bridge found, select (1,0) without checking others further.

examples.input: Current vertex: 2, unvisited edges: (2,3) only. Graph: path 0-1-2-3.
examples.trace: Step 1: List edges from vertex 2: only (2,3). Step 2: Check (2,3): it is a bridge (removal disconnects). Step 3: No other edges available, so select this bridge.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Construction & Degrees
ladders.desc: Determine if a graph remains connected after edge removal.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept underpins bridge detection. The intuition: a bridge is defined by its effect on connectivity, so we need to check if removing an edge disconnects the graph. Connectivity means there's a path between any two vertices. Step-by-step: 1) Remove the edge in question from the graph representation. 2) Perform DFS/BFS from any vertex. 3) If all vertices are visited, graph is still connected; otherwise, disconnected. Time: O(V+E). Space: O(V) for visited array. Connection to L2: Bridge detection uses this idea efficiently via low values without explicit removal. Common mistakes: Not handling the original graph copy, incorrect visited reset, or assuming connectivity in directed graphs. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list, temporarily remove edge, run DFS, and compare visited count to total vertices.

examples.trace: Step 1: Remove edge (1,2). Remaining edges: (0,1), (2,3). Step 2: Start DFS from vertex 0. Visit 0, then 1. Step 3: Vertices visited: {0,1}. Step 4: Total vertices: 4. Step 5: Not all visited → disconnected.

Step 1: Remove edge (0,1). Remaining edges: (0,2), (1,2). Step 2: Start DFS from vertex 0. Visit 0, then 2, then 1. Step 3: Vertices visited: {0,1,2,3? wait 3 not in graph}. Actually vertices are 0,1,2 only. Step 4: All vertices visited → connected.

testCases.input: Graph: 1 vertex, no edges, remove nothing

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Edge Removal / Traversal
ladders.desc: Visit all vertices reachable from a starting point.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in Fleury's algorithm for bridge detection and connectivity checks. The intuition: traversal explores the structure of the graph by following edges. DFS goes deep first, using recursion or a stack; BFS goes level by level, using a queue. Step-by-step for DFS: 1) Mark start vertex as visited. 2) For each neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS. Time: O(V+E). Space: O(V) for visited storage plus recursion stack. Connection to L3: Connectivity checks rely on traversal to see if all vertices are reachable. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark visited, infinite loops in cycles, or stack overflow in deep recursion. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list, visited boolean array, and recursive function or explicit stack.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: 1 (not visited) → go to 1. Step 3: At 1, mark visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited), 2 (not) → go to 2. Step 4: At 2, mark visited. Neighbors: 1 (visited), 3 (not) → go to 3. Step 5: At 3, mark visited. Neighbors: 2 (visited) → backtrack. Step 6: Backtrack to 2, then 1, then 0 → done.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbors: 1 and 2. Suppose go to 1 first. Step 3: At 1, mark visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited) → backtrack. Step 4: Back at 0, go to 2. Step 5: At 2, mark visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited) → backtrack. Step 6: Done.

testCases.input: Graph: 1 vertex, no edges, start at 0
Graph: 3 vertices, edges: none, start at 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Euler Path & Graph Basics
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, edges, degrees, and Euler paths.
ladders.explanation: An Euler path visits every edge exactly once. It exists if exactly 0 or 2 vertices have odd degree (undirected) or specific indegree/outdegree conditions (directed). Hierholzer finds it by merging circuits efficiently.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[], [], []]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): append 1 to graph[0] → graph[0]=[1]; append 0 to graph[1] → graph[1]=[0]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): append 2 to graph[1] → graph[1]=[0,2]; append 1 to graph[2] → graph[2]=[1]. Final: graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0,2], graph[2]=[1].

Step 1: Initialize graph = [[], [], [], []]. Step 2: Add (0,1): graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0]. Step 3: Add (0,2): graph[0]=[1,2], graph[2]=[0]. Step 4: Add (1,2): graph[1]=[0,2], graph[2]=[0,1]. Step 5: Add (1,3): graph[1]=[0,2,3], graph[3]=[1]. Final: as above.
